articles.title: How Docker Desktop Networking Works Under the Hood (2022)
articles.summary: Docker Desktop uses a helper Linux VM to forward traffic to the outside world, avoiding issues with VPN policies that prevent containers from accessing resources like internal registries. The VM requests an address using DHCP, forwards the request to a virtual ethernet switch, and then sends ARP requests to discover the gateway's IP address. When a container establishes a TCP connection, Docker Desktop creates a virtual TCP stack on the host, accepting connections and exchanging packets with the remote machine.

articles.title: Can gzip be a language model?
articles.summary: A compressor such as gzip can be used to generate language models by predicting the probability of each word in a given text based on its context. This is achieved through the compression-prediction equivalence, where every prediction model is inherently a compressor and all compression algorithms are prediction models. The algorithm uses a sliding window of 32 KiB to find matches against the recent text in the window, encoding continuation that echoes something already in the window as a cheap back-reference instead of literal bytes. This allows for the generation of coherent text with high probability, making it possible to prime a model and generate language models such as tiny Shakespeare.

A developer's workflow can be likened to a series of related tasks that ultimately lead to a single objective, much like yak shaving which involves sharpening an axe to chop down a tree before moving on to find a better stone. This process of simplifying and streamlining one's work is known as "yak shaving" and has parallels in the way developers approach their projects.

articles.title: Has AI already killed self-help nonfiction books?
articles.summary: Has AI already killed off self-help nonfiction books by devouring their sales at an alarming rate, with some subcategories declining by as much as 57% in just one year? The decline is attributed to the widespread adoption of conversational AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT, which have led to a significant decrease in print book sales. According to data from Publishers Weekly, adult nonfiction was down 9% in the first three months of 2026 compared to the same period last year. This trend is particularly concerning for self-help books, which have seen their sales decline by as much as 26.3%.

articles.title: Stop Using JWTs
articles.summary: JWTs should not be used for keeping users logged in as they are not designed for this purpose and are not secure. Regular cookie sessions provide better security and flexibility, and there is a more suitable tool available for authentication. JWTs have specific design limitations that make them unsuitable for long-lived sessions, making it difficult to implement stateless authentication.

articles.title: TIL: You can make HTTP requests without curl using Bash /dev/TCP
articles.summary: You can make HTTP requests without curl using Bash by opening a TCP socket directly from the shell, which allows you to write a small HTTP request to it by hand. This requires setting up a container or service name on a Docker network and swapping in your own host and port. The Connection: close header is essential for closing the connection after receiving a response, and this feature is not available in POSIX systems like Linux.

This finding suggests that readers can learn valuable lessons from the strip about the value of integrity in everyday life.

articles.title: zlib-rs in Firefox
articles.summary: Zlib-rs in Firefox uses zlib-rs for gzip compression, providing performance and safety advantages. The integration process took two years, with the Mozilla engineers first discussing it in summer 2024 before finally shipping a patch in May. Switching to zlib-rs is not entirely trivial due to changes in algorithms used at different compression levels, which can result in slight output byte and length variations. This change was necessary to prevent symbol clashes, but required configuration updates for the Firefox test suite.

articles.title: How memory safety CVEs differ between Rust and C/C++
articles.summary: Memory safety CVEs in Rust differ from those in C/C++ due to how potential vulnerabilities related to memory unsafety are treated. While Rust's unsafe keyword can cause undefined behavior, it is not the only way to experience it, and general vulnerabilities can occur without it. In contrast, C/C++ tends to be more memory safe because of their focus on robustness, which includes checks for memory access that would otherwise lead to undefined behavior. This difference in approach may not be immediately apparent, but it has significant implications for developers who compare the two languages based on CVE data.
